summary:
Transfacial proportion analysis typically divides the face into simple upper, middle, lower third sections instead of finer horizontal or vertical subdivisions as suggested earlier; this approach aligns better with standard clinical practice guidelines focusing primarily on these major regions during assessments such as facial aesthetics evaluations. To directly answer your query regarding specific numbers without overcomplicating it further based on current understanding within medicine—two primary transverse planes are often considered sufficient when discussing 'segments' across most professional literature related to facial anatomy and surgery techniques today. Agent1 concludes that while there might exist some theoretical frameworks proposing additional sub-divisions including up-to-five levels horizontally, they aren't widely adopted clinically due mainly because their application lacks clear utility compared against established methods emphasizing broader zones defined through fewer but clearer distinctions between areas above/below certain landmarks identified via visual inspection alone suffice adequately under normal circumstances encountered routinely among healthcare professionals working closely with patients seeking aesthetic improvements or corrective procedures involving complex reconstructive surgeries requiring precise alignment relative only two main axes running fronto-posteriorly along midline nasal bridge region versus temporal mandibular junction boundary demarcation line respectively delineated visually upon initial examination prior any surgical intervention being planned out meticulously thereafter according strict adherence towards preoperative planning protocols designed specifically targeting individual patient needs accordingly ensuring optimal outcomes achievable postoperatively thereby minimizing risks associated therewith appropriately managed throughout entire course from diagnosis till final recovery phase completion successfully achieved thus far reported consistently amongst majority cases handled effectively utilizing aforementioned simplified yet robust framework currently accepted broadly across various disciplines involved herein ranging orthodontics maxillofacial prosthesis cranio-facial trauma care etc... 

In summary,
- Face division usually follows a trichotomy model vertically split into top-middle-bottom thirds
- Five-level subdivision isn’t commonly used practically in routine clinical settings Given its complexity vs simplicity offered by traditional method. 
The core point here was simplifying down to just mentioning typical use case of dividing face into roughly equal parts vertically since higher level detail wasn't strictly required per original Q&A format constraints provided initially.
